name: M.S. in Internet Engineering

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: This is a focused, coherent program: the 19 referenced courses form a clear networking spine - Internet/higher-layer protocols (ECE 637), computer communication networks (ECE 783), broadband networks (ECE 639), network management and security (ECE 638), NFV/data-center virtualization (ECE 681), cloud/IoT (ECE 683), and 5G wireless (ECE 645) - grounded by programming (CS 505, CS 602 Java) and random-signal analysis (ECE 673), with management electives (MGMT 620, MIS 625) giving a professional edge. Progression is real (ECE 673/683 feed ECE 783; ECE 637/683 feed ECE 638).
dimensions.suggestion: Formalize the evident spine into a stated required core (protocols, networks, security) plus electives so the coherent design is explicit rather than implied.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: deepestPrereqChain is 2 (e.g., ECE 683/637 to ECE 638) with sensible layering, and missingCourseCount is 3 (ECE 251, 333, 481) - undergraduate references. Clarity is hurt by contaminated prerequisite fields: CS 602 and ECE 638 both have description text embedded in the prerequisite string.
dimensions.suggestion: Clean the CS 602 and ECE 638 prerequisite fields and label ECE 251/333/481 as undergraduate preparation.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes appear in the data, so the specific networking/security competencies this focused degree confers are not stated.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish outcomes such as 'design, secure, and evaluate large-scale IP and cloud networks' and map them to ECE 637/638/681/783.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: Unlike the other ECE M.S. programs, statedTotalCredits is explicitly 30, giving real load transparency; however creditReconcileDelta is null and requirementGroups 0, so the split between required core, electives, and the seminar (ECE 791) is not visible.
dimensions.suggestion: Keep the stated 30 credits but add the credit breakdown across core, technical electives, and management electives.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The page is clean: descriptions are present and specific for all 16 sampled courses (no blanks), titles clearly signal the networking theme, and a total of 30 credits is stated. The remaining gap is the absence of a labeled plan of study.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a term-by-term or core/elective plan-of-study table to complement the already-strong descriptions and stated total.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: Content is markedly current: NFV and data-center virtualization (ECE 681), cloud and IoT networking/security (ECE 683), 5G network design (ECE 645), and network management/security (ECE 638), all central to modern Internet infrastructure, plus e-commerce management (MIS 625) for professional context.
dimensions.suggestion: Add an explicit software-defined-networking (SDN)/network-automation course to complete the modern networking stack alongside ECE 681.

descriptionQuality.justification: Descriptions are specific and complete across all sampled courses (e.g., ECE 681 on network-function algorithms, ECE 639 on ATM/SONET/congestion control), with no blank or placeholder entries in the sample.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated as course codes with sensible layering (ECE 783 requires ECE 673 and ECE 683), but clarity is reduced by CS 602 and ECE 638 embedding description prose in the prerequisite field and by three undergraduate references (ECE 251/333/481).

missingTopics.topic: Software-defined networking (SDN) and programmable data planes
missingTopics.rationale: ECE 681 covers NFV but there is no course on SDN controllers, OpenFlow/P4, or intent-based networking, now foundational to Internet engineering.

missingTopics.topic: Applied cryptography and modern web security
missingTopics.rationale: ECE 638 addresses network security generally, but there is no dedicated applied-crypto/TLS/zero-trust course for securing Internet-scale applications.
With CS 505/602 for programming, the program lacks a distributed-systems or big-data course covering consensus, replication, and stream processing behind modern Internet services.
CS 602 Java and MIS 625 touch web business, but there is no modern full-stack/REST-microservices course reflecting how Internet applications are actually built.
ECE 636 provides a lab, yet no course focuses on Internet measurement, telemetry, and observability for large-scale service performance.

emergingTrends.rationale: Service meshes and container networking are the operational reality of Internet systems; ECE 681/683 should incorporate cloud-native networking.
Edge compute co-located with 5G/6G radios is reshaping the Internet edge; ECE 645/683 could add MEC and edge-service placement.
ML for traffic engineering, anomaly detection, and intent-based operation is emerging; ECE 638/783 could add network-AI content leveraging ECE 673.
Perimeterless security models are becoming standard; ECE 638/683 should evolve toward zero-trust architectures for cloud and IoT.
Protocol evolution (QUIC, HTTP/3, TLS 1.3) is changing Internet traffic; ECE 637's protocol course should be modernized to cover them.

topSuggestions: Add a software-defined-networking / programmable-data-plane course to complete the modern networking stack alongside ECE 681.
Publish program learning outcomes tied to the ECE 637/638/681/783 spine.
Formalize the implicit networking spine into a stated required core plus electives, and add a core/elective credit breakdown to the stated 30 credits.
Introduce an applied-cryptography/zero-trust security course and a distributed-systems course to broaden beyond packet-level networking.
Clean the contaminated CS 602 and ECE 638 prerequisite strings.
